1.synthesis: Pharisees and scribes from Jerusalem observe that Jesus's disciples eat with defiled (unwashed) hands and challenge him on it (vv.1–2). Mark pauses to explain the hand-washing tradition for his Gentile readers (vv.3–4) before giving Jesus's counter-charge: they have abandoned God's commandment to uphold the tradition of men (v.8). The specific example is Corban — a vow mechanism that allowed someone to declare goods "dedicated to God" and thereby withhold financial support from aging parents, directly nullifying the Fifth Commandment (vv.10–12). Isaiah's verdict is applied: this people honors God with lips while the heart is far off; their worship is vain because it teaches human doctrines as divine commands (vv.6–7, quoting Isaiah 29:13). The polemic is not against tradition per se but against the specific abuse of tradition to override written Torah.
1.historical_context: The "tradition of the elders" (paradosis tōn presbyterōn, v.3) was the body of oral interpretation the Pharisees believed had been passed down alongside the written Torah from Sinai — later codified in the Mishnah (ca. 200 CE). Mark's extended explanation of Jewish purity customs (vv.3–4) is aimed at Gentile readers unfamiliar with them, distinguishing Mark's audience from Matthew's Jewish-Christian readers (who receive no such gloss). Hand-washing before meals was a Pharisaic extension of the priestly purity laws of Exodus 30:17–21; the rabbis applied the priests' temple-purity requirements to all lay Israelites as a way of making every table a form of the altar. The qorbān practice is documented in Josephus (Against Apion 1.167): goods dedicated to the Temple could not be transferred to any other purpose — even parental support — without violating the vow. The leaders used this legal mechanism to circumvent the commandment to honor parents.
1.literary_structure: The pericope has a chiastic structure: challenge (vv.1–2) → Isaianic diagnosis (vv.6–7) → principle of priority (v.8) → specific example (vv.9–12) → summary conclusion (v.13). The Corban case is the concrete instance of the abstract principle: oral tradition weaponized against written commandment. Mark's parenthetical explanation (vv.3–4) for Gentile readers is itself a Markan literary marker — his Gospel was composed for a community outside Palestine, and his pauses to explain Jewish customs (7:3–4; 12:42; 15:42) indicate where his intended audience would need orientation. The double use of "reject/make void" (vv.9, 13: atheteite and akyrountes) frames the entire polemic as a legal argument about which authority supersedes which.
1.theological_application: The Isaianic diagnosis ("this people honors me with their lips, but their heart is far from me," v.6) names the central spiritual danger of any mature religious tradition: the form of devotion becoming dissociated from its animating reality. Lip-worship is real worship of a kind — the words, the gestures, the observances are present — but the heart has moved elsewhere, toward social standing, institutional loyalty, or personal advantage. The Corban case is the diagnostic specimen: a person using the language of consecration to God to protect wealth from family obligation. Religion in service of self-interest produces exactly this inversion: the most devout-sounding act (dedicating goods to God) used to violate the most basic human obligation (honoring parents).
1.christology: Jesus's counter-charge operates from a position of interpretive authority over the entire tradition: he distinguishes authentic divine command from human addition and pronounces which supersedes which. No scribe had this authority — scribes interpreted the tradition; they did not stand outside it and judge it against God's own intention. The claim implied in "you reject the commandment of God and hold to the tradition of men" (v.8) is that Jesus knows God's commandment well enough to identify when tradition departs from it — the kind of knowledge available only to one who has access to the Father's own intention. This is the same authority demonstrated in the Sermon on the Mount's "you have heard ... but I say to you" formula: Jesus over tradition, not tradition over Jesus.

14.synthesis: Jesus calls the crowd and delivers a compact teaching: nothing that enters a person from outside can defile; only what comes out from within defiles (vv.14–15). In private the disciples ask for explanation; Jesus expresses mild frustration at their incomprehension (v.18). He explains: what enters the mouth goes through the digestive system and out — "thus he declared all foods clean" (v.19, Mark's editorial parenthetical, absent from Matthew). What comes out of a person comes from the heart: evil thoughts, sexual immorality, theft, murder, adultery, coveting, wickedness, deceit, sensuality, envy, slander, pride, foolishness — all these defile (vv.21–23). The list is a moral catechism of interior vices, covering violations of every major commandment category.
14.historical_context: Mark's editorial parenthetical "thus he declared all foods clean" (v.19) is the most theologically significant editorial note in the Gospel; it draws an explicit conclusion from the principle that Matthew does not make — perhaps reflecting Mark's Roman community's engagement with the food-law question (Romans 14; Acts 10:15). The list of thirteen vices in vv.21–22 has been compared to Hellenistic virtue-and-vice lists (catalogues of sins) common in philosophical and Jewish moral literature, but Jesus's list is distinctive in rooting all external sins in the interior kardia (heart) — the OT seat of will, motivation, and character. The word porneia (sexual immorality, v.21) covers the broad Levitical prohibitions; pleonexia (coveting/greed, v.22) echoes the Tenth Commandment.
14.literary_structure: The pericope moves from public teaching (vv.14–15) to private explanation (vv.17–23) — the same pattern of public parable / private interpretation that characterized Mark 4's parable discourse. The disciples' failure to understand (v.18: "are you also without understanding?") is a recurring Markan motif of discipleship incomprehension that will escalate through the Gospel. The editorial declaration of all foods clean (v.19) breaks the narrative frame momentarily — Mark the narrator steps forward to name what Jesus's teaching implies for his community's practice. The vice list's thirteen items move from inner attitudes (evil thoughts, deceit, envy, pride) to outward actions (theft, murder, adultery, slander) — confirming that the categories are not separate: inner vice precedes and produces outer offense.
14.theological_application: "For from within, out of the heart of man, come evil thoughts ..." (v.21). The teaching relocates the problem of human sin from external contact (ritual impurity, dietary violation) to internal formation. This is not a dismissal of bodily life or physical discipline — it is a reorientation of religious energy toward the source rather than the symptoms. The Pharisaic tradition had multiplied external purity requirements; Jesus identifies that the same human being who carefully washes hands before meals nurtures murder, deceit, and pride in the heart without any concern. The remedy implied is not more external regulation but the transformation of the heart — what Jeremiah's new covenant promises (Jeremiah 31:33): the law written on the heart rather than merely observed in the hand.
14.christology: The parenthetical "thus he declared all foods clean" (v.19) attributes to Jesus an authority that no scribe or prophet had claimed: the abrogation of a Levitical dietary code that had defined Israel's identity for over a millennium. This is not interpretation but declaration — katharizōn, the Greek participle, marks it as a decisive act of the teacher's own authority rather than an inference from prior texts. The one who declares all foods clean is the same one who enters Gentile territory (7:24) to heal a Syrophoenician child — the principle and its practice belong together. Mark places the clean-foods declaration immediately before the Gentile mission episodes of 7:24–8:10, confirming that the two are continuous: purity redefined as moral, not ethnic or dietary, opens the path to universal inclusion.

24.synthesis: Jesus enters a house in the region of Tyre and wants no one to know — but cannot remain hidden (v.24). A woman whose daughter has an unclean spirit immediately hears of him and falls at his feet; she is a Greek, Syrophoenician by birth (vv.25–26). She begs him to cast the demon from her daughter. Jesus: "Let the children be fed first, for it is not right to take the children's bread and throw it to the dogs" (v.27). She answers: "Yes, Lord; yet even the dogs under the table eat the children's crumbs" (v.28). Jesus: "For this statement you may go your way; the demon has left your daughter" (v.29). She returns home and finds the child lying on the bed, the demon gone (v.30). Mark's account is starker than Matthew's: no "great faith" commendation, just the clean logic of the woman's argument producing the miracle.
24.historical_context: Tyre was a Phoenician coastal city, part of the Roman province of Syria — thoroughly Gentile territory. The woman's description as "Greek" (Hellēnis, Gentile by religion and culture) and "Syrophoenician by birth" (distinguishing Phoenician Syrians from Libyan Syrians) is Mark's precise ethnic placement. The diminutive kynarion (little dog, household dog) rather than the street-dog of a Jewish epithet suggests a metaphor of domestic inclusion: house-dogs under the table share proximity to the household even if they are not the children. The children-first logic ("let the children be fed first," v.27) implies a sequence: Israel first, then the Gentiles — the same priority structure as Matthew 10:5–6 but with the temporal qualifier "first" opening the door to what follows. The woman's answer takes the metaphor and finds the inclusion within it: crumbs are not the meal but they are real provision from the same table.
24.literary_structure: The episode is the hinge between the clean/unclean teaching of vv.1–23 and the Decapolis healing/feeding of vv.31–8:10: Jesus has just declared all foods clean; he now heals a Gentile child; then he enters Gentile territory and feeds a Gentile crowd. The theological logic is sequential and deliberate. The woman's persistence (she falls at his feet, v.25; she answers his boundary statement with an argument, v.28) mirrors the pattern of petitioners who move through initial rejection to miracle by the force of their faith-request. Mark omits Matthew's commendation of "great faith" — for Mark, the statement that won the miracle is not praised as exemplary but simply reported as effective. Her word prevails: the demon has left (v.29), confirmed by the mother's return to find the child resting.
24.theological_application: The woman's answer — "Yes, Lord; yet even the dogs under the table eat the children's crumbs" (v.28) — is a model of faith that accepts the boundary named and finds grace within it rather than arguing against it. She does not dispute the priority of Israel; she argues that her daughter's need can be met from what overflows that priority without diminishing it. This is the posture that finds favor: not entitlement ("I deserve the children's bread") but humble argument from abundance ("the table produces more than the children require"). The miracle follows the argument — for this statement, not for any extraordinary faith-act or purification, the demon departs. The kingdom responds to the word that rightly reads the logic of grace.
24.christology: Jesus "could not be hidden" (v.24) even in Gentile territory — the messianic secret fails before a Gentile woman because the power of the kingdom cannot be contained by geographical or ethnic boundaries. His response to her argument ("for this statement, go your way," v.29) reveals a christology of responsive authority: he grants her request on the basis of her word's theological coherence, not on the basis of prior relationship or covenant membership. The one who declared all foods clean (v.19) now declares a Gentile child clean of unclean spirits (v.29) — both actions are expressions of the same expanding sovereignty that refuses to be bounded by the categories the tradition has imposed.

31.synthesis: Returning from Tyre through Sidon and down to the Sea of Galilee in the Decapolis region, Jesus is brought a deaf man with a speech impediment (vv.31–32). Jesus takes him aside privately from the crowd, puts his fingers in the man's ears, spits and touches the man's tongue, looks up to heaven, sighs deeply, and says: "Ephphatha" — "Be opened" (vv.33–34). The man's ears are opened; his tongue is released; he speaks plainly. Jesus charges them to tell no one, but the more he charges, the more zealously they proclaim it: "He has done all things well. He even makes the deaf hear and the mute speak" (vv.36–37). The healing is in Gentile Decapolis; the crowd's proclamation echoes Genesis 1:31's "it was very good" — here applied to a new-creation act of mercy.
31.historical_context: The Decapolis ("ten cities") was a league of Hellenistic cities east and south of the Sea of Galilee, predominantly Gentile — confirmed as the setting by the healing of the Gerasene demoniac (5:1–20) in the same region. The tactile healing method (fingers in ears, spittle on tongue) was associated with ancient healing practices: saliva was regarded as having therapeutic and even magical properties in both Jewish and Gentile contexts (b.Bava Batra 126b mentions spit-healing; Tacitus and Suetonius record Vespasian healing with saliva). Jesus's looking up to heaven and sighing deeply before speaking suggest a pneumatic quality to the moment — the breath of the healer, the Spirit-empowered word. "Ephphatha" is the Aramaic transliteration Mark preserves (as he did with talitha koum, 5:41), giving the reader the precise word that opened deaf ears. The crowd's acclamation ("he has done all things well") uses the same verb as the LXX's "God saw that it was good" in Genesis 1.
31.literary_structure: The healing is private (Jesus takes him aside, v.33) but the outcome is public despite the command to silence (v.36). This is a pattern in Mark (1:44–45; 5:43; 7:36): the messianic secret is repeatedly overwhelmed by the evidence of healing, creating a narrative tension between Jesus's desire to control the timing of his disclosure and the irresistible nature of his deeds. The Isaiah resonance is explicit in the crowd's acclamation: the deaf hearing and the mute speaking is precisely the sign of the messianic age in Isaiah 35:5–6 — the healing enacts the restoration prophecy without citing it, trusting the reader to hear the echo. Mark places the healing immediately before the second feeding (8:1–10), creating a sequence: Gentile territory → deaf healed → mute speaks → four thousand fed.
31.theological_application: The crowd's conclusion — "He has done all things well; he even makes the deaf hear and the mute speak" (v.37) — is the reaction that cannot be silenced: the evidence exceeds the command. The messianic secret in Mark is not about preventing praise but about preventing premature messianic enthusiasm before the Cross defines what kind of messiah Jesus is. Here the Gentile crowd praises without political agenda — they are simply astonished at the quality of the restoration. Their acclamation resonates with Genesis 1:31's verdict on creation, suggesting that what Jesus does in healing is not merely humanitarian but cosmologically significant: each healing is a restoration of the goodness God intended for creation before its corruption.
31.christology: "Ephphatha" — "Be opened" (v.34). The single Aramaic word, addressed to deaf ears and a bound tongue, achieves what years of hearing loss and inability to speak could not reverse. This is the creative word of Genesis 1 — God spoke and it was; Jesus speaks and the body obeys. The sigh before the word (estenaxen, "groaned deeply") is one of the most evocative emotional details in Mark's Gospel: the healer carries the weight of the condition he is about to reverse, not as an actor performing a miracle but as one who feels the wrongness of deafness and silencing before he speaks them away. The sign-value for Mark's community: the one who opens deaf ears also opens the ears of those who hear his gospel.
